storage_extensions.usage: mmap sorted_l_orderkey.bin. Pass 1: parallel sequential scan for run-length aggregation.

files.sorted_l_qty_uint8_format: uint8_t[59986052]; value = (uint8_t)l_quantity; integer in [1,50]; row order matches sorted_l_orderkey.bin

storage_extensions.usage: mmap sorted_l_qty_uint8.bin instead of sorted_l_quantity.bin (double). Accumulate as int32_t running sum (no overflow: 50 * 60M = 3B, fits int32). Compare sum > 300 as integer. Store sum_qty as double for output (cast final int32 sum to double). Reduces Pass 1 from 720MB to 300MB total (sorted_l_orderkey 240MB + sorted_l_qty_uint8 60MB).

join_order.notes: Pass 2 iterates over 624 qualifying keys directly — no lineitem scan
~624 probes; pages pre-faulted in parallel before the serial probe loop
~624 probes; pages pre-faulted in second parallel sweep after orows are known

filters.notes: Applied after accumulating full run-sum for each orderkey in Pass 1

scan.access_pattern: sequential (single pass, 300MB total vs 720MB in iter_2)

data_structures.pass1_thread_local: struct BoundaryState { int32_t first_key, last_key; int32_t first_partial_sum, last_partial_sum; int64_t first_end_pos, last_start_pos; } per thread — tiny, L1-resident. Use int32_t for sum accumulation (max sum per key = 50*60M/15M = 200, well below INT32_MAX).
data_structures.pass1_qualifying_keys: std::vector<QualifyingKey> per thread, QualifyingKey = { int32_t key; int32_t sum_qty_int; int64_t start_pos; int64_t end_pos; }
data_structures.pass1_inline_hash_prefault: Within the parallel scan, when a thread emits an interior qualifying key, immediately issue volatile read of o_hash_keys[hash32(key) & o_hash_mask] to pre-fault the hash table page. This overlaps page-fault latency with the 20ms scan.
data_structures.prefault_hash_arrays_parallel: After boundary merge: open a new #pragma omp parallel section. Each thread takes a subset of the 624 qualifying keys, computes hash position h = hash32(key) & mask, and reads one byte from o_hash_keys[h] and one byte from o_hash_vals[h] (volatile read). Faults in the relevant pages of the 256MB o_orderkey_hash. This converts ~1248 serial page faults (5us each = 6ms) into 64-thread parallel work (0.1ms total).
data_structures.prefault_c_hash_parallel: In the same parallel sweep as above: also pre-fault the c_custkey_hash pages. Compute c_hash_pos = hash32(o_hash_keys[h] ... but we don't know custkey yet. SKIP c_custkey_hash prefault at this stage — do it after orow is known.
data_structures.prefault_orders_cols_parallel: After serial hash probes produce 624 orow values: open another #pragma omp parallel section. Each thread takes a subset of the 624 orow values, does volatile reads of o_custkey_col[orow], o_orderdate_col[orow], o_totalprice_col[orow], and c_hash_keys[hash32(o_custkey_col[orow]) & c_hash_mask] to pre-fault those pages. Converts ~1872 serial page faults into parallel work.
data_structures.output_buffer: std::vector<Result> (~624 entries), sorted and top-100 emitted

passes.name: pass1_parallel_sorted_scan_aggregate_uint8
passes.description: Parallel morsel-driven scan of sorted_l_orderkey (int32, 240MB) + sorted_l_qty_uint8 (uint8, 60MB) = 300MB total (vs 720MB in iter_2). Each thread scans contiguous chunk [row0, row1). Accumulate run-length sums as int32_t (max value per key well below INT32_MAX). When key changes: if cur_sum > 300, emit QualifyingKey to thread-local vector and IMMEDIATELY issue a volatile read of o_hash_keys[hash32(key) & o_hash_mask] to pre-fault the hash table page for this qualifying key. Record boundary state for first/last key as before.
passes.key_change: Use int32_t cur_sum (not double); compare cur_sum > 300 (integer). Cast to double for output: qk.sum_qty = (double)cur_sum.
passes.inline_prefault: When interior qualifying key found: volatile int32_t dummy = o_hash_keys[hash32(key) & o_hash_mask]; (void)dummy; — pre-faults the hash page during the parallel scan, amortizing the cost.

passes.name: pass1_boundary_merge
passes.description: Same logic as iter_2: merge adjacent thread boundary states, collect all qualifying keys (interior + boundary-spanning) into global_qualifying vector. Expected ~624 qualifying keys.

passes.name: prefault_hash_pages_parallel
passes.description: After boundary merge, open a short #pragma omp parallel section over the 624 qualifying keys. Each thread processes its share of qualifying keys: for each key k, compute h = hash32(k) & o_hash_mask, then issue: volatile int32_t d1 = o_hash_keys[h]; volatile int32_t d2 = o_hash_vals[h]; (void)d1; (void)d2; This maps the pages of the 256MB o_orderkey_hash into the process's page table in parallel. With 64 threads × ~20 pages each × ~5us per page fault from hot page cache = ~5us total (parallel). All 1248 hash pages are now in the process page table before the serial probe loop.

passes.name: pass2_hash_probes_serial
passes.description: Serial pass over ~624 qualifying keys. For each: probe o_orderkey_hash to get orders_row (orow). Pages already in page table from prefault step: each probe is now L3 cache miss (~100ns) not a page fault (~5us). Collect (key, orow) pairs into a small array. Store resulting orow values for next prefault step.

passes.name: prefault_orders_column_pages_parallel
passes.description: After getting all 624 orow values, open another short #pragma omp parallel section. Each thread processes its share of 624 orow values: for each orow, issue volatile reads of o_custkey_col[orow], o_orderdate_col[orow], o_totalprice_col[orow], c_name_col[orow_for_cust * 26] (use orow to get custkey, then c_hash for customer row). Actually, to prefault c_custkey_hash we need custkey which requires reading o_custkey_col[orow] first. Simplify: parallel prefault of o_custkey_col[orow], o_orderdate_col[orow], o_totalprice_col[orow] for all 624 orows. Then also prefault c_custkey_hash first probe position using the now-available custkey values.

passes.name: pass2_column_reads_serial
passes.description: Serial pass over 624 (key, orow) pairs. Read o_custkey[orow], o_orderdate[orow], o_totalprice[orow] (pages now hot), probe c_custkey_hash to get crow (pages hot), read c_name[crow*26]. Build Result structs with sum_qty from qualifying key records.

parallelism.notes: New: two short parallel prefault sweeps (prefault_hash + prefault_orders_cols) between boundary merge and serial probes. These are brief (<<1ms each) but eliminate the serial page-fault overhead from build_joins.

indexes.purpose: join_lookup: qualifying l_orderkey -> orders row index
indexes.access: mmap, MADV_RANDOM, ~624 total probes; pages pre-faulted before probe loop
mmap, MADV_RANDOM, ~624 total probes; pages pre-faulted in second sweep

aggregation: linear_run_length with int32_t accumulator (cast to double for output)

output.date_decode: convert o_orderdate (int32 days since 1970-01-01) to YYYY-MM-DD for output only (100 rows)

optimization_notes: Two root causes identified from iter_2's 66ms profile. (1) main_scan (20ms): Reading sorted_l_quantity.bin as double (8 bytes/row = 480MB) wastes 7x bandwidth — TPC-H l_quantity is always an integer in [1,50], fitting in uint8 (1 byte/row = 60MB). A new storage extension (sorted_l_qty_uint8.bin, 60MB) built via type-narrowing reduces Pass 1 data from 720MB to 300MB (sorted_l_orderkey 240MB + sorted_l_qty_uint8 60MB), targeting a proportional reduction in main_scan time (~8ms vs 20ms). (2) build_joins (15ms): The serial probe loop over 624 qualifying keys probed into large hash tables (o_orderkey_hash 256MB, c_custkey_hash 32MB) plus random-access into orders column files — all with pages NOT in the process's page table after the sequential scan evicts them from TLB. Each probe triggers a minor page fault (~5us from hot page cache). 624 probes x ~6 sequential page faults each = ~15ms serial overhead. Fix (Category B/C): two parallel prefault sweeps. First sweep (64 threads, 624 qualifying keys): touch each o_orderkey_hash key/value slot in parallel, mapping pages into the process page table in ~0.1ms total. Second sweep (after orow values known): touch o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice, and c_custkey_hash slots in parallel. Additionally, within Pass 1's parallel scan, each thread that finds an interior qualifying key immediately touches the corresponding hash table page — amortizing hash prefaults across the 20ms scan. Together these eliminate ~12ms of serial page-fault latency from build_joins.
